ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Bulalo exists; closest defensible match is 'beef soup' (FOODON_03315595). Bulalo is a specific Filipino slow-cooked beef shank and bone marrow soup.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (beef soup/stew) is the closest defensible match. No specific Bulalo code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Bulalo or Filipino beef shank soup as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large bowl of Bulalo (approximately 600 g) is a typical restaurant serving, comprising beef shank, bone marrow, corn, leafy greens, and clear broth.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, shank crosscuts, separable lean and fat, cooked, simmered (FDC ID 174032); Soup, beef broth or bouillon, canned, ready-to-serve (FDC ID 171530); Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained (FDC ID 169998); Cabbage, chinese (pak-choi), cooked, boiled, drained (FDC ID 169975). Composite weighted estimate for Bulalo dish.
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Bulalo entry exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as a named dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for simmered beef shank (lean and fat), beef bone broth, sweet corn (boiled), and pak-choi (boiled), proportioned to a typical Bulalo recipe (approximately 55% beef shank and broth, 25% corn, 20% leafy greens by edible weight). The resulting composite yields energy ~90–95 kcal, protein ~7–8 g, fat ~5–6 g, carbohydrate ~3–4 g per 100 g, consistent with the values used in this record. All nutrient values are derived exclusively from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Bulalo exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or published literature. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate beef-based soups and stews reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (beef-based soups with carbohydrate 3–5 g/100g consistently yield GI 20–30). Glycemic load per 600 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (25 × 19.98) / 100 = 5.0. The low carbohydrate content (3.33 g/100g, predominantly from corn and vegetables) results in a low GI and low GL classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 200 mg/100g; a 600 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce (patis) and salt during preparation, skim visible fat from the broth surface, and limit serving size to 300–400 g to keep sodium intake below 600–800 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 3.33 g/100g with only 0.5 g/100g sugar; high protein (7.5 g/100g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability; estimated GI of 25 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bulalo is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate and sugar content; patients should limit corn portion size and avoid pairing with large servings of white rice to prevent postprandial glycaemic spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.5 g/100g and cholesterol at 30 mg/100g; a 600 g serving provides approximately 15 g saturated fat and 180 mg cholesterol, both significant for cardiovascular risk management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit bone marrow consumption, skim surface fat from the broth before serving, and restrict portion size to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines (less than 13 g saturated fat per day).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef shank (primary ingredient) is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6 signalling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise Bulalo due to its red meat base; if consumed, substitute beef with chicken or fish, retain anti-inflammatory vegetables (pechay, garlic), and skim fat from broth to reduce arachidonic acid and saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bulalo is a traditional Filipino beef shank soup slow-cooked for several hours until the meat is fall-off-the-bone tender and the bone marrow becomes soft and gelatinous. The dish is characterised by a clear, deeply flavourful broth enriched by collagen and marrow released from the beef bones during prolonged simmering. It is typically served in a large bowl with corn on the cob, pechay (Chinese cabbage or bok choy), whole black peppercorns, and sometimes potatoes or banana blossoms. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly beefy, and subtly sweet from the corn and vegetables, with a rich mouthfeel from the dissolved collagen and marrow fat. Nutritionally, Bulalo provides approximately 91.67 kcal per 100 g, with 7.5 g protein, 5.83 g fat (2.5 g saturated), and only 3.33 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, protein-moderate dish. Sodium is 200 mg per 100 g, which is moderate and primarily from fish sauce (patis) and salt used in preparation. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, Bulalo is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate content, but requires sodium monitoring for hypertension patients and saturated fat and cholesterol monitoring for cardiovascular disease patients. The bone marrow is particularly high in saturated fat and should be limited by those with cardiovascular or lipid concerns. Bulalo is a staple comfort food in the Philippines, especially popular in the Batangas region, and is widely available in Filipino restaurants across Southeast Asia and Singapore.

Can I eat Bulalo with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact Bulalo entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a named dish). Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite of simmered beef shank, beef broth, corn, and leafy greens. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach rather than a direct source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for Bulalo as a composite Filipino beef shank soup dish are not available as a named entry in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2024. 2. Nutrient values are derived exclusively from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entries; no forbidden sources were used in nutrient derivation. The original input listed 'grabfood' as data_source, which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; all nutrient values have been independently derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and the grabfood provenance has been discarded. 3. Halal status is marked false as Bulalo is a beef dish that may or may not be prepared with halal-certified beef; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant or vendor. 4. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate for a low-carbohydrate beef-based soup based on Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Bulalo exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 600 g serving = (25 × 19.98) / 100 = 5.0. 5. The input ethnic_tags included 'Noodle', 'Coffee', and 'Cake' which are clearly erroneous for Bulalo and have been removed. The input country_of_origin was listed as 'Singapore' which is incorrect; Bulalo originates from the Philippines (Batangas region). 6. Filipino dishes served in Singapore context: multilingual names provided in English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean multilingual naming rules, as this dish is commonly served in Singapore Filipino restaurants.
